coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen migration in products during the microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization of spirulina platensis
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Dandan
Wang, Yuang
Gao, Ying
Lyu, Yinong
Lin, Yunhao
Pan, Yueshen
Zhu, Lin
Zhu, Yuezhao
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen has a vital influence on the properties of the microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization (MHTC) products of Spirulina platensis (SP). The effects of hydrothermal temperature (140–220 °C) and time (1–4 h) on the product distribution and nitrogen migration of SP in MHTC were studied. Increasing temperature led to an increase in the carbon content, and a decrease in the nitrogen content in hydrochar. Protein-N was the major nitrogen-containing species in hydrochar. The total nitrogen in liquid phase increased significantly with increasing temperature. Carbon dots were found to be one of the valuable products in the liquid phase. Higher temperatures improved the amine-N level and reduced the quaternary-N content in carbon dots. A close correspondence was found between the N-containing species and the luminescence centers of carbon dots. A possible nitrogen migration mechanism was proposed to provide guidance for the potential application of the products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spirulina platensis
Microwave assisted hydrothermal carbonization
Nitrogen transformation
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Properties of hydrochar and N transformation in hydrochar
N Transformation in SP-CDs and the effect of N doping on the photoluminescence properties of SP-CDs
Potential mechanism of N migration in MHTC

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20201365); Aero-engine Thermal Environment and Structure Key Laboratory of Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (CEPE2019008); Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Process Enhancement and New Energy Equipment Technology; the Top-notch Academic Program Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions; State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering (KL20-06); Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (19KJB610001). The authors also thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the support of XPS test.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen distribution in the MHTC products.
N-containing species distribution in the hydrochar.
Nitrogen species and total nitrogen concentration in liquid products (a) temperature range; (b) time range.
Possible nitrogen migration mechanism of SP in MHTC process.
Ultimate analysis, the atomic ratio, and yield of the feedstock and hydrochar.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The SD is based on 3 samples.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen migration in products during the microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization of spirulina platensis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen has a vital influence on the properties of the microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization (MHTC) products of Spirulina platensis (SP). The effects of hydrothermal temperature (140–220 °C) and time (1–4 h) on the product distribution and nitrogen migration of SP in MHTC were studied. Increasing temperature led to an increase in the carbon content, and a decrease in the nitrogen content in hydrochar. Protein-N was the major nitrogen-containing species in hydrochar. The total nitrogen in liquid phase increased significantly with increasing temperature. Carbon dots were found to be one of the valuable products in the liquid phase. Higher temperatures improved the amine-N level and reduced the quaternary-N content in carbon dots. A close correspondence was found between the N-containing species and the luminescence centers of carbon dots. A possible nitrogen migration mechanism was proposed to provide guidance for the potential application of the products.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomass is a carbon–neutral source of energy for reducing carbon emissions and mitigating climate change (Abbasi and Abbasi, 2010). The development of innovative biomass energy technologies has gained increasing attention in recent decades. Microalgae are an emerging energy feedstock that has high photosynthetic efficiency, strong environmental adaptability and high harvest yield. Hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) is one of the most promising technologies for the resource utilization of microalgae because the reaction conditions are remarkably mild and wet feedstocks do not need pretreatment by drying (Heilmann et al., 2010). The HTC of microalgae mainly generates liquid and solid products. (Balou et al., 2020; Park et al., 2018). Algae are usually rich in nitrogen, for example, the nitrogen content in Spirulina platensis (SP) is as high as 10.95%. The high nitrogen content in algae feedstock may affect the physiochemical properties of the hydrothermal products and even cause environmental pollution during their applications.

Given that the content and existing forms of nitrogen in the carbonization products of algae are strongly related to their properties and potential applications, nitrogen migration during the HTC of microalgae has been studied. Xiao et al. (Xiao et al., 2019) investigated the nitrogen transformation of spirulina in HTC, and found that N in the bio-char was transformed to liquid and bio-oil as the temperature raised because the polymerization reactions of pyrrole-N and pyridine-N were promoted. Leng et al. (Leng et al., 2021) reviewed the transformation of nitrogen-containing species in hydrochar derived from nitrogenous biomass and concluded that nitrogen was fixed in the solid hydrochar via Millard and Mannich reactions. Few studies have reported on the migration behavior of nitrogen during the HTC of microalgae. Insights can be obtained from research on the transformation of nitrogen in the HTC of other nitrogen-containing biomass feedstocks (e.g., soy protein, sludge, and etc.). Chacón-Parra et al. (Chacón-Parra et al., 2021) found that nitrogen transformation mainly occurred in the aqueous phase, and acetic acid limited the ammonia formation from organic nitrogen compounds in the hydrothermal treatment of compound soy protein. Zhuang et al. (Zhuang et al., 2017) studied the conversion of nitrogen during the hydrothermal treatment of sewage sludge and suggested that 20% of nitrogen in sewage sludge was converted into hydrochar, while the remaining 80% evolved to liquid and gaseous phases. Similar results were found in Li et al.’s research (Li et al., 2020). Furthermore, high reaction temperature and long retention time were conducive to the transformation of nitrogen in raw materials to NH4+-N in liquid phase, and the influence of temperature is more obvious (Ahmad et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019).

Microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization (MHTC) has been rarely investigated in studies on nitrogen migration of biomass in hydrothermal process. Microwave-assisted heating has the advantages of high heating efficiency and low energy consumption and its non-thermal effect improves the fluorescence performance of carbon dots (CDs) (Liu et al., 2021). Aqueous products are usually treated as wastewater or diluted as fertilizer for plant (Leng et al., 2020c). CDs were also found in the aqueous product of MHTC of biomass (Liu et al., 2020). CDs are a promising nanomaterials because of their low toxicity and good biocompatibility and thus have attracted increasing research interest (Ai et al., 2021; Guo et al., 2021). N doping likely contributes to the formation of new energy levels, and improves the photoluminescence quantum yield (Dsouza et al., 2021). Thus far, limited reports have been found on the nitrogen migration of CDs in the MHTC of microalgae. Therefore, nitrogen migration in the MHTC of SP should be comprehensively investigated.

Therefore, this paper aims to explore the transformation of nitrogen during the MHTC of SP at 140–220 °C, and focus on the distribution of nitrogen-containing species in hydrochar and liquid phase. The effect of nitrogen-containing functional groups on the photoluminescence properties of the obtained CDs was analyzed to improve product quality. A deeper understanding of the mechanism of nitrogen migration and transformation in the whole MHTC process of SP was proposed. Results suggested that regulating and controlling N transformation during MHTC would improve the quality of products. This research would provide basic data for the efficient and clean utilization of microalgae via MHTC.

Feedstock SP powder was obtained from Wudi Lvqi Bioengineering Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). The powder was dried at 60 °C for 8 h without further pretreatment. The SP has a protein content of 69.65% as measured by Kjeldahl nitrogen method and a nitrogen content of 10.95%.

For the MHTC experiment, the SP powder was mixed with deionized water at the ratio of 1:50. The amount of SP for each experiment was 1 g. The mixture was placed in a Teflon-lined reaction vessel on a microwave workstation (XO-SM100; Xianou). Experiments were carried out at different reaction temperatures (140–220 °C) for different durations (1–4 h) with a fixed microwave power (300 W). The heating-up time was relatively short (3–8 min) and thus had limited effect on the total reaction time (1–4 h). When the set reaction time was over, the heating power became 0, the fan was turned on, and the mixture was cooled to 60 °C for 20–30 min. The cooling time was not included in the reaction time. When cooling to 60 °C, the solid residue and liquid products in the vessel were separated by a high-speed centrifuge. The solids were dried at 105 °C for 24 h, ground and stored for subsequent tests. Parts of the liquid products were collected and stored in a refrigerator for characterization. the remaining liquid products were purified by dialysis to obtain CDs solution. The liquid product was placed in a dialysis bag with molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Dalton. The bag was placed in a beaker filled with deionized water. The deionized water in the beaker was changed every 4 h, and the dialysis lasted for 48 h. The CDs solution was pre-frozen in a refrigerator and dried in a vacuum freeze dryer. After 48 h, loose CDs powder was obtained. The experiments for each condition were repeated at least three times.

The percentages of C, H, N and S in the sample were determined by an Vario EL cube elemental analyzer (Germany). Elemental information and chemical structures were studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer) with X-ray source of Al Kα rays (hv = 1486.6 eV). The vacuum of the analysis chamber was higher than 5.0E-7 mBar. The pass energy and step size were 100 and 50 eV for survey scan and 1 and 0.1 eV for narrow scan, respectively. The surface functional groups and chemical bonds of hydrochar and CDs were measured via Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 6700, Thermo Scientific, USA) in the region of 4000–750 cm−1 with KBr pellet technique. Total nitrogen (TN) concentration was analyzed by alkaline potassium persulfate digestion UV spectrophotometric method according to the Chinese standard HJ636-2012 (Water quality-determination of total nitrogen-alkaline potassium persulfate digestion UV spectrophotometric method). Ammonium-N (NH4 +-N) was obtained by distillation-neutralization titration method according to the standard HJ537-2009 (Water quality-determination of ammonia nitrogen-distillation-neutralization titration). Nitrate-N (NO3 –-N) was tested by UV-spectrophotometry according to the standard HJ/T346-2007 (Water quality-determination of nitrate-nitrogen Ultraviolet spectrophotometry). Nitrite-N (NO2 –-N) was tested by spectrophotometric method according to the standard GB7493-1987 (Water quality-determination of nitrogen (nitrite)-Spectrophotometric method). The concentration of the total organic nitrogen (TON) was difficult to be measured directly by instrument and was thus calculated as follows: TON = TN − (NH4 +-N + NO3 –-N + NO2 –-N). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the CDs were recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometry (FL6500, PerkinElmer, USA). After parameter optimization, the parameters of the PL measurement were set as follows: flash power (kW) = 20; frequency (Hz) = 100; excitation wavelength (nm) = 220–460; excitation slit (nm) = 10; emission start wavelength (nm) = 240; emission end wavelength (nm) = 700; emission slit (nm) = 10; and emission scan speed (nm/min) = 24000.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of hydrochar and N transformation in hydrochar
ce:section.ce:para: The ultimate analysis and the atomic ratio of the raw feedstock and the obtained hydrochar are shown in Table 1. With increasing reaction temperature from 140 °C to 220 °C (for1 h), the C content considerably reached 49.03 ± 0.15% to 64.15 ± 0.16%. The C content continued to increase from 55.54 ± 0.19% to 59.73 ± 0.10% as the reaction time was prolonged from 1 h to 4 h (at 180 °C). Elevated reaction temperature and longer reaction time promoted the carbonization degree of hydrochar, and the influence of temperature was relatively greater. Polymerization and aromatization are the major reactions that contribute to the formation of carbonaceous products in hydrochar (Chen et al., 2017). The O content in hydrochar dropped significantly from 26.28 ± 0.16% to 12.45 ± 0.11% with increasing temperature due to dehydration and decarboxylation (Marin-Batista et al., 2019). In the temperature range of 140–220 °C, the contents of N and S decreased from 10.42 ± 0.10% to 6.53 ± 0.06%, and from 0.55 ± 0.04% to 0.38 ± 0.03%, respectively. The hydrothermal temperature had a significant effect on the desulfurization and denitrification of SP, which is beneficial to the use of hydrochar as fuel because it can reduce NO x and SO x emissions. The atomic ratios of H/C and O/C are important indicators used to evaluate the carbonization degree of SP. With increased hydrothermal temperature and prolonged time, the H/C and O/C of the hydrochar decreased from 1.81 to 1.47 and from 0.40 to 0.15, respectively. The C content in this study was significantly higher than that obtained from the HTC of SP investigated by Zhang et al. at the same hydrothermal temperature range (180 °C–220 °C) (Zhang et al., 2020); meanwhile, the S content and O/C ratio were lower. The difference in the results could be due to different heating methods, and the non-thermal microwave effect might improve the carbonization degree and desulfurization effect of SP.

Although higher hydrothermal temperature and longer time are beneficial to increase the C content and decrease the N content in hydrochar, the yield of hydrochar needs to be considered. Hydrothermal temperature and time greatly influenced the yield of hydrochar. The continuous increase in the temperature (140–220 °C) sharply decreased the yield of the hydrochar from 69.3 ± 3.5% to 15.3 ± 2.4%. The increase in the reaction time (1–4 h) also decreased yield from 36.8 ± 0.5% to 20.8 ± 1.3%. This finding is because SP is rich in protein and is easily decomposed in a hydrothermal environment, while lignin, cellulose, etc. are more recalcitrant and tend to form hydrochar. For solid fuel, a trade-off exists between fuel yield and fuel environment friendliness (residual N and S contents).

XPS and FTIR were applied to study the evolution of N in hydrochar in MHTC. According to the ultimate analysis in Table 1, the denitrification effect of MHTC was obvious, a considerable part of N was retained in the hydrochar (for example, N content in hydrochar obtained at 180 °C was more than 8%); this finding may be due to the N-containing aromatic rings formed in the Maillard reaction in the hydrochar (Li et al., 2019b). The results of ultimate analysis reflect the variation in total N in hydrochar; N-containing functional groups in the hydrochar are closely related to its application in catalysis, energy storage, and pollutant adsorption (Leng et al., 2020a). Thus, N-containing species were identified by XPS. The high resolution N1s spectrum was deconvoluted into four peaks at 398.4 ± 0.2 eV, 399.8 ± 0.1 eV, 400.6 ± 0.1 eV and 401.3 ± 0.1 eV, corresponding to different chemical states of N: pyridine-N, protein-N, pyrrole-N, and quaternary-N, respectively (Ahmad et al., 2019; Xiao et al., 2019) (see Supplementary Material). The results of peak fitting are shown in Fig. 2 . Protein-N accounted for the highest N content (more than 60%) in all the N-containing components mainly due to the sufficient protein in the feedstock. With increasing temperature and reaction time, the relative content of protein-N decreased continuously from 72.6% to 63.8%, and the relative contents of pyridine-N (from 7.0% to 14.8%) and quaternary-N (from 1.7% to 7.3%) increased. The content of pyrrole-N had the smallest variation range. This finding indicates that proteins were decomposed continuously in MHTC from 140 °C to 220 °C. In particular, the content of protein N decreased by 5.3% (at 180 °C–200 °C), which was identified as the biggest drop in all adjacent temperature increments. Hence, the optimal hydrolysis temperature of proteins was 180 °C–200 °C. The N content in the liquid phase showed the largest gain in the corresponding temperature range (Fig. 1). In addition, the relative contents of pyridine N and quaternary N increased with increasing temperature. Among the four nitrogen components, the content of quaternary-N was the lowest but had the greatest increase from 1.7% to 7.3% with increasing temperature. High temperatures promoted the formation of quaternary-N and pyridine-N, and the former is more thermally stable (Ahmad et al., 2019). Reaction time had less effect on the distribution of N-containing species in the hydrochar than temperature. Extending the reaction time by 3 h (at 180 °C) led to a 2.6% reduction in protein-N content, and fewer changes in pyridine-N and pyrrole-N contents.

The surface functional groups of the hydrochar were also analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy (see Supplementary Material). The broad band at 3600–3100 cm−1 is ascribed to the overlay stretching of O–H and N–H, and the intensity weakened with increasing temperature (140–220 °C), indicating that high temperature caused more intensive dehydration reaction. The peaks at 2925 and 2848 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of C–H in methylene groups (Xiao et al., 2019). The intensity of the peaks was significantly enhanced at 220 °C. The characteristic peaks at 1624 and 1526 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibration of C = O (amide I) and deformation vibration of N–H (amide II), respectively (Liu et al., 2020). The two peaks show the existence of protein structure, and their intensity weakened, indicating that the proteins were continuously broken down and transformed with increasing temperature. This trend is consistent with the change in protein-N content with temperature (Fig. 2). The intensity of the absorption peak at 1021 cm−1 due to C–O/C–O–C stretching decreased in the hydrochar derived from 140 °C to 220 °C. Some small peaks in this region almost disappeared at 220 °C. The oxygen-containing functional groups were largely removed by dehydration and thermal decomposition, corroborating the declining trend of O content from the ultimate analysis of the hydrochar. Based on the characterization data, N in hydrochar is closely correlated with protein in raw materials, and the N content decreased with increasing temperature and time. However, a considerable amount of nitrogen was fixed in hydrochar, including pyridine-N, protein-N, pyrrole-N, and quaternary-N; among which, pyridine-N and quaternary-N were more stable. The hydrochar obtained by HTC generally has the advantages of uniform particles, easy control of porosity, and abundant functional groups on the surface (Yu et al., 2017). Activated algal hydrochar could be used as adsorbent for municipal sewage treatment (Mahima et al., 2021). The effect of N-containing functional groups on the adsorption capacity of algae-derived hydrochar is the future direction.

Liquid products rich in organic and inorganic N are usually treated as wastewater. The variations in N-containing species in liquid products with hydrothermal temperature and time are shown in Fig. 3 . The total nitrogen (TN) in liquid can be categorized into organic nitrogen (Org-N) and inorganic nitrogen including ammonium nitrogen (NH4 +-N), nitrate nitrogen (NO3 –-N) and nitrite nitrogen (NO2 –-N). Temperature has a great influence on the N-containing species in the liquid products. As shown in Fig. 3a, the TN concentration increased from 1000 ± 39 mg/L to 1964 ± 32 mg/L with temperatures ranging from 140 °C to 220 °C, which was largely attributed to the hydrolysis of protein and the dissolution of inorganic nitrogen. Among the several N-containing species measured, except at 200 °C, the concentration of NH4 +-N was the highest, followed by NO3 –-N and Org-N, and the concentration of NO2 –-N remained very low with little change. The concentration of Org-N peaked (593 ± 37 mg/L) at 200 °C and then decreased. Correspondingly, the concentration of NH4 + -N declined slightly from 608 ± 42 mg/L to 572 ± 28 mg/L at 160–200 °C and then increased to 820 ± 41 mg/L at 220 °C. The decarboxylation of amino acids to amides increased the concentration of Org-N, but further increase in temperature (e.g., 220 °C) resulted in the formation of NH4 +-N from unstable amides through deamination (Ekpo et al., 2016). N&O-heterocyclic compounds were also present in a large quantity in the liquid products of HTC at 200 °C (Gai et al., 2015). The formation of N&O-heterocyclic compounds included the dehydration of piperazines, the direct cracking of N-heterocycle amino acids, the Maillard reactions, and so on (Leng et al., 2020b). The concentration of NO3 –-N increased steadily with increasing temperature, and reached the peak (676 ± 29 mg/L) at 220 °C. The concentration of NO3 –-N was slightly higher than that of Org-N, which is different from the low concentration of NO3 –-N in a previous study (Xiao et al., 2019). NO2 –-N was not discussed in detail because of its low content (3–4%). Compared with temperature, the increase in the reaction time had little effect on the distribution of N-containing species in liquid products. As the reaction time was prolonged from 1 to 4 h, the concentrations of TN and Org-N displayed a modest growth, and the concentration of NH4 +-N, NO3 –-N as well as NO2 –-N varied minimally with time (Fig. 3b). The liquid products are rich in organic and inorganic substances, and are by-products of the MHTC process of biomass. Recycling of liquid phase could improve the yield of hydrochar, and the diluted liquid product could also be used for microalgae cultivation (Leng et al., 2020c). The effect of liquid phase circulation on the N content and properties of the products is worthy of study in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N Transformation in SP-CDs and the effect of N doping on the photoluminescence properties of SP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs are valuable products in the liquid phase. The optical properties of CDs can be modified by N doping. The effects of nitrogen source, nitrogen content, and nitrogen chemical state have been documented in literature (Hu et al., 2020; Pillar-Little and Kim, 2017; Wu et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2021). The types and quantities of N-containing species in SP-CDs (CDs derived from SP) and their influence on the fluorescence properties were investigated in the following section.

The chemical states of N-containing species of SP-CDs were analyzed using XPS. The N atomic percentage almost doubled from 6.58% to 12.89% with increasing temperature from 140 °C to 180 °C. The N atomic percentage remained unchanged as the temperature increased from 180 °C to 200 °C. From 200 °C to 220 °C, the N atomic percentage increased from 12.89% to 13.44%. Changes in temperature, especially below 200 °C, had a greater impact on N atomic percentage in SP-CDs. Reaction time could not compare with temperature in terms of the effect on the N atomic percentage of SP-CDs. Preparation conditions determine the degree of N incorporation. The high resolution XPS spectrum of the N 1 s could be fitted with four peaks centered at 398.5 ± 0.1, 399.5 ± 0.1, 400.4 ± 0.1, 401.3 ± 0.1 eV, which are assigned to pyridine-N, amine-N, pyrrole-N and quaternary-N, respectively (Li et al., 2019a; Pillar-Little and Kim, 2017) (see Supplementary Material). Quaternary-N (also known as graphitic-N) corresponds to N-doping in the carbon core, while pyrrole-N and amine groups are related to surface doping. Pyridine-N can exist either in the core or on the surface of the CDs (Dsouza et al., 2021). The distribution of N-containing species in SP-CDs under different preparation conditions is shown in Fig. 4 . The content of amine-N that dominated in all prepared SP-CDs accounts for more than 50%. The presence of amide surface groups in SP-CDs was corroborated by FTIR (see Supplementary Material). High temperature and long reaction time contributed to the formation of quaternary-N, that is, to N-doping in the carbon core.

The photoluminescence characteristics of SP-CDs were explored as a function of N-containing species. The number and intensity of fluorescence centers (A, B, and C) varied with the preparation conditions of SP-CDs in the excitation-emission spectra (see Supplementary Material). The emission peaks of all SP-CDs are visible in the range of 430–450 nm with different numbers of fluorescent centers. For the SP-CDs prepared at 180 °C-1 h, the fluorescent center A becomes visibly stronger, and the intensity of the three fluorescent centers are close, indicating that 180 °C for 1 h was the transitional condition from the B- and C-dominated fluorescent centers to the A-dominated fluorescent center. The fluorescent center A had the optimum excitation at ∼ 340 nm, and the fluorescence was believed to originate from π − π* transitions in the carbon core (Dsouza et al., 2021). The fluorescent center A became stronger with increasing temperatures. The increase in reaction time also led to a similar pattern. This finding showed that the fluorescent center A was associated with the degree of carbonization of CDs. From the XPS results in Fig. 4, the content of quaternary-N mainly existing in the carbon core increased with increasing temperature and time, and the trend is consistent with that of the fluorescent center A. In particular, the increasing rate was more obvious when the temperature exceeded 160 °C or the time was more than 1 h. Under the corresponding conditions, the intensity of the fluorescent center A was enhanced dramatically. The intensity of the fluorescent center A was relevant to the quantity of quaternary-N in the carbon core. The emission at 430–450 nm excited by high-energy ultraviolet light (∼280 and ∼ 240 nm) was marked as the fluorescent centers B and C, respectively. The intensity of the two fluorescent centers became weaker and the fluorescent center B even practically disappeared with increasing hydrothermal temperature and time. These emissions excited with higher energy were likely to be associated with surface states, such as pyrrole-N, amine groups and O-containing groups (carbonyl and carboxyl groups) (Carbonaro et al., 2018). The fluorescent center B was attributed to the nN 2p–σ* electronic transition of amino groups (Lai et al., 2020). The FTIR spectra revealed the existence of C − N and C = N groups (see Supplementary Material). The increase in hydrothermal temperature and time continuously decreased the amine-N content (Fig. 4), indicating the correlation between amine-N and the fluorescent center B. Overall, the fluorescence emission of SP-CDs prepared at low temperatures was mainly derived from the electronic transition of surface state, while that of SP-CDs prepared at high temperatures mostly originated from the band gap transition of the carbon core. In summary, the N-containing species in SP-CDs are closely related to the fluorescence emission. Compared with cellulose-rich biomass such as peanut shell and cotton stalk (Liu et al., 2020), CDs prepared by protein-rich SP had better yield and fluorescence characteristics and did not easily absorb water in air under similar reaction conditions. This result could be due to the effect of self-doping of nitrogen and N-containing species on the fluorescent centers. Furthermore, the adjustable emission features were obtained only by changing the preparation conditions from the same precursor, which further demonstrated that MHTC is a simple and promising method to prepare CDs from SP. N-doped CDs from microalgae have been used as fluorescent inks for anti-counterfeiting (Guo et al., 2021). Additional research on the application of CDs is under development.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential mechanism of N migration in MHTC
ce:section.ce:para: The possible N migration mechanism of SP in MHTC is proposed in Fig. 5 . First, N in the raw material can be divided into nitrogen from inorganic salts and nitrogen from organic matter, mainly proteins. The dissolution of nitrate and nitrite forms nitrate and nitrite nitrogen in the liquid phase. Protein (wt. %, 69.65%) is the main component of SP and the main source of N in various products. Protein is hydrolyzed into amino acids and then amines and amides by decarboxylation. The hydrolysates and derivatives of proteins are the source of organic nitrogen in liquid phase. The concentration of Org-N in liquid phase increases within 160–200 °C, which is the suitable temperature range for amino acid hydrolysis. However, the concentration significantly decreases at 220 °C mainly due to the conversion of amino acids into NH4+-N by deamination at high temperatures. Elevated temperatures considerably increase the total nitrogen in liquid (89.7% at 220 °C). Meanwhile, carbohydrates (cellulose and hemicellulose) are hydrolyzed into reducing sugars such as glucose and fructose. Glucose was dehydrated to produce oxygenate compounds. N&O-heterocyclic compounds are formed through the Maillard reaction. The Maillard reaction is a complex process between carbonyl compounds (reducing sugars) and amino compounds (amino acids and proteins) and yields brown and even black macromolecules (Gai et al., 2015). In addition, pyrrole-N and pyridine -N are derived from the recombination and cyclization of amino acids, and continuously converted into more stable quaternary-N with increasing temperature. All of the above compounds and intermediates could generate nanoscale CDs, which have photoluminescence properties, and could be dissolved in liquid phase through complex polymerization, condensation and carbonization. Most of the Maillard reaction products, indissoluble cellulose, and some intermediate products combined with ash are converted into hydrochar through complex polymerization and carbonization reactions (Wang et al., 2021). The N-containing species distributed in SP-CDs and hydrochar are similar: pyridine-N, amine-N, protein-N, pyrrole-N and quaternary-N. Despite the similar ultimate composition and N-containing species, the properties of hydrochar and CDs differ possibly because of variations in size and structure. In summary, the full understanding of N transformation mechanism in products during the MHTC process of SP would lead to improvements in properties and enhancement in application.

The temperature and time of MHTC are the key factors affecting the distribution of nitrogen in products, the migration of N-containing species and the properties of the products. High temperatures contribute to the denitrification of hydrochar, increase the TN concentration in liquid, and enhance N-doping in SP-CDs. Hydrolysis, dehydration, polymerization and Maillard reaction promoted the nitrogen transformation among the products. The fluorescent centers of SP-CDs are greatly affected by the preparation conditions. Amine-N and quaternary-N are correlated with the electronic transitions and fluorescent centers. The desired products can be obtained by regulating N transformation in MHTC.

Dandan Guo: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yuang Wang: Investigation, Visualization. Ying Gao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision. Yinong Lyu: Resources, Supervision. Yunhao Lin: Investigation, Methodology. Yueshen Pan: Investigation, Resources. Lin Zhu: Resources, Supervision. Yuezhao Zhu: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20201365); Aero-engine Thermal Environment and Structure Key Laboratory of Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (CEPE2019008); Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Process Enhancement and New Energy Equipment Technology; the Top-notch Academic Program Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions; State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering (KL20-06); Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (19KJB610001). The authors also thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the support of XPS test.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2022.126968..
